summary: Gruber excoriates Medium and other websites for their persistent on-screen sharing bars — which he calls 'dickbars' — that obstruct content and undermine readability, especially on mobile devices. He argues that Safari already provides built-in sharing functionality that elegantly appears and disappears as needed, making these overlays redundant. He singles out Medium for particular criticism because the platform positions itself as reader-friendly, yet its UI choices contradict that identity. Gruber traces the problem to SEO-driven decision-making that prioritizes 'engagement' metrics over actual reading experience, and argues that the same mindset produced bloated JavaScript-heavy pages that then require AMP to fix. He concludes that Medium's reputation has shifted from 'nice to read' to 'oh crap.'
key_points: Persistent sharing bars and 'Open in App' banners cover actual content, making the last 1-2 lines unreadable on mobile
Safari already provides sharing and app-install prompts (Smart App Banners) in user-friendly, dismissible ways — these overlays are redundant
Higher 'engagement' from dickbars is likely driven by accidental taps rather than genuine user intent
SEO-driven thinking created the bloated pages that now require AMP to fix — the same people are pushing dickbars
Medium's adoption of these patterns is especially disappointing because the platform's entire value proposition is readability
money_quotes: Every Medium site displays an on-screen 'sharing' bar that covers the actual content I want to read.
To me these things are as distracting as having someone wave their hand in front of my face while I try to read.
A website should not fight the browser. Let the browser provide the chrome, and simply provide the content.
The SEO folks are the same dopes who came up with the genius strategy of requiring 5-10 megabytes of privacy-intrusive CPU-intensive JavaScript on every page load that slows down websites.
Now they come to their teams and say, 'Our pages are too slow — we gotta move to AMP so our pages load fast.'
I don't expect better from most websites. I do expect better from Medium.

When people click a URL and see that it's a Medium site, their reaction should be 'Oh, good, a Medium site — this will be nice to read.' Right now it's gotten to the point where when people realize an article is on Medium, they think, 'Oh, crap, it's on Medium.'
key_insight: Websites that fight the browser's built-in chrome with redundant, content-obscuring UI overlays betray their readers for illusory engagement metrics — and Medium's adoption of this pattern is particularly damning because readability is its entire reason for existing.
